Bending light.<[silence:40ms]> Different tools.<[silence:40ms]>

The words lens and prism both trace to Latin—lens (lentil, for its shape), prisma (something sawed, for its sharp edges).<[silence:40ms]> Both reshape light, but where a lens guides it smoothly, a prism splits it apart like a knife through a ribbon.<[silence:40ms]>

A lens curves light inward or outward, focusing rays into a single point—a magnifying glass burning paper, or eyeglasses sharpening blurry text.<[silence:40ms]> Its power lies in precision: a camera lens bends every color of light the same way, keeping images crisp.<[silence:40ms]> A prism does the opposite.<[silence:40ms]> Strike white light through its angled glass, and it fractures into rainbows, revealing hidden colors.<[silence:40ms]> Scientists use prisms to analyze starlight, uncovering what distant suns are made of.<[silence:40ms]>

One unifies.<[silence:40ms]> One divides.<[silence:40ms]>
Three related subjects are light refraction tricks, how telescopes exploit lenses, and why diamonds sparkle like prisms.<[silence:40ms]>
clean_story:
Bending light. Different tools.

The words lens and prism both trace to Latin—lens (lentil, for its shape), prisma (something sawed, for its sharp edges). Both reshape light, but where a lens guides it smoothly, a prism splits it apart like a knife through a ribbon.

A lens curves light inward or outward, focusing rays into a single point—a magnifying glass burning paper, or eyeglasses sharpening blurry text. Its power lies in precision: a camera lens bends every color of light the same way, keeping images crisp. A prism does the opposite. Strike white light through its angled glass, and it fractures into rainbows, revealing hidden colors. Scientists use prisms to analyze starlight, uncovering what distant suns are made of.

One unifies. One divides.
Three related subjects are light refraction tricks, how telescopes exploit lenses, and why diamonds sparkle like prisms.
